comments:
Summary:
This paper presents a thorough empirical study of Learning Hidden Unit Contributions (LHUC) for unsupervised adaptation of DNN acoustic models, and introduces a Speaker-Adaptive-Training variant (SAT-LHUC). The authors evaluate LHUC and SAT-LHUC across four standard corpora (TED, AMI, Switchboard, Aurora4), study many practical aspects (amount/quality of adaptation data, layer choices, hyperparameters, complementarity with fMLLR, one-shot use, sequence-trained models), and propose a factorised approach to combine speaker and environment transforms.

Strengths:
- Comprehensive experimental evaluation across multiple, diverse datasets and model architectures (DNN, CNN, Maxout/CNN), demonstrating method robustness.
- Practical focus: shows LHUC works with very small amounts of unsupervised data (10s), is complementary to fMLLR, and can adapt sequence-trained models without catastrophic forgetting.
- Clear description of LHUC and SAT-LHUC algorithms and how they are integrated into training/decoding (including math for gradients and SI/SD sampling scheme).
- Novel and useful exploration of factorised speaker/environment interpolation and of SAT-style training for LHUC.
- Results are compelling: consistent WER reductions across tasks and speakers; SAT-LHUC often improves further over test-only LHUC.

Weaknesses / Limitations:
- Although the paper cites auxiliary-feature approaches (i-vectors, speaker codes), direct empirical comparison in the same experimental conditions is limited; readers would benefit from head-to-head comparisons (especially given the practical overlap in intent). The authors note related work but more explicit comparisons would strengthen claims of complementarity or superiority.
- Practical deployment costs are not quantified: storage and runtime overheads for per-speaker LHUC parameters, and adaptation time/latency (especially for one-shot or online scenarios) are not reported. This matters for real systems.
- Some hyperparameter choices (e.g. γ SI/SD ratio, learning rates for r) and their sensitivity are discussed, but a more systematic ablation (or clearer guidance) would help practitioners.
- Theoretical discussion is largely intuitive (basis function view); formal analysis of adaptation capacity or limits of LHUC (e.g. when features are severely mismatched) is limited, though experiments partially address this.

Suggestions for improvement:
- Add explicit comparisons (or matched-condition baselines) using i-vectors / speaker codes / fDLR where feasible, or at least summarize quantitatively how LHUC compares when those are applied in the same pipeline.
- Report memory/storage and per-speaker parameter counts, and average adaptation runtime (per speaker) to help assess deployability.
- Provide more detailed ablation on γ and other hyperparameters (or an easy recipe for selecting them).
- If possible, release code/recipes or provide sufficient configuration details to improve reproducibility (the work uses Kaldi recipes in places; linking to those or to scripts would help).

Conclusion:
This is a solid, well-executed empirical paper addressing an important practical problem in acoustic model adaptation. It advances the state of knowledge by showing LHUC is effective, efficient with small amounts of unsupervised data, complementary to feature-space methods, and extendable to SAT and sequence-trained models. With additional practical deployment details and more direct comparison to auxiliary-feature approaches it would be even stronger. I recommend acceptance and consideration for an oral presentation.
